summary: hpsetup CLI fetches code from unrelated.xyz host and overlays it onto @heroui-pro packages
details: The package brands itself as a setup CLI for commercial HeroUI Pro packages (@heroui-pro/react, heroui-native-pro) but downloads its payloads from https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz, a random numeric.xyz domain unrelated to the vendor's published domain (heroui.com). On invocation (e.g. `npx hpsetup`), src/download.js fetches a tarball from `${PROXY}/api/tarball/${product.slug}/${version}` (constants.js: PROXY = "https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz"), pipes the response through tar-extract, and `cpSync`s the contents directly into the consumer's `node_modules/@heroui-pro/react` (or `heroui-native-pro`). Whoever controls 932324.xyz can ship arbitrary JavaScript that ends up resident at well-known package paths inside the installer's project. Two corroborating behaviors increase the harm surface: (1) src/trust.js declares OUR_PACKAGES = ["@heroui-pro/react", "heroui-native-pro", "@zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk"] and silently writes those names into pnpm's `onlyBuiltDependencies` / `allowBuilds` and Bun's `trustedDependencies`, authorizing lifecycle scripts for an unrelated IBM credential-handling SDK in projects whose maintainers had explicitly opted into a default-deny posture; (2) after tar-extracting, `cleanPostinstall` in src/download.js removes any `dist/postinstall` directory and rewrites the overlaid package's package.json to delete its `postinstall` script, leaving a node_modules state that no lockfile attests to and that hides the lifecycle hook from later auditing. The destination is unpinned, unverified (no hash/signature check), and the publisher does not match the advertised vendor — the canonical fetch-and-overlay dropper shape.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
